items.q: What are the four pillars of food security?
items.correct: Availability (enough food exists), access (people can obtain it), utilisation (nutritional quality and absorption) and stability (consistent over time) — defined by the FAO.
items.misconception: Food security just means producing enough food.
items.random_wrong: Food security means storing food for emergencies.
items.why: Most modern famines occur where food is available but inaccessible (Amartya Sen's entitlement theory). Production alone doesn't deliver security — distribution, affordability and nutrition all matter.

items.q: In a tropical rainforest nutrient cycle, where is most of the nutrient stored?
items.correct: In the biomass (living plants). Litter is rapidly decomposed by warm humid conditions, and soil is leached — so cycling between biomass and litter dominates.
items.misconception: Most nutrients in a rainforest are stored in the soil — that's why plants grow so well.
items.random_wrong: Most rainforest nutrients are stored in animals.
items.why: Rainforest soils are notoriously thin and infertile. The Gersmehl model shows biomass as the dominant store — which is why slash-and-burn agriculture collapses quickly when biomass is removed.

items.q: How do waves erode a cliff?
items.correct: Through hydraulic action (water compressing air in cracks), abrasion (waves throwing rocks against cliffs), attrition (rocks smashing against each other), and solution.
items.misconception: Waves erode cliffs by pushing water against the rock until it breaks.
items.random_wrong: Cliffs erode because of acid rain dissolving them.
items.why: Water pressure alone is rarely enough. The main mechanisms involve compressed air and rocks acting as tools — students underestimate how erosion needs material to do the wearing.
The answer examiners want is: Richter. “Mercalli” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The San Andreas Fault is what type of margin?
items.why: Correct answer: Conservative. “Constructive” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the magnitude of the 2010 Haiti earthquake?
items.why: The real figure is 7 Richter. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
